turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion of the Court in case 21-1195, Bittner v. United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: This case concerns a federal statute that require certain individuals to file annual reports with the Federal Government about their foreign bank accounts. The question we face boils down to this. Does someone who non-willfully fails to file a timely or accurate annual report commit a single violation subject to a single $10,000 statutory penalty or does that person commit separate violations and incur separate $10,000 statutory penalties for each account not timely or properly recorded within a single report. We took this case because the Circuits have disagreed on the right answer. The Ninth Circuit has interpreted the statute the first way. The Fifth Circuit the second. The right answer matters particularly for immigrants to this country and Americans living abroad both of whom often hold foreign bank accounts. It matters too because under the second view adopted by the Fifth Circuit, penalties for even unintentional violations of the law can quickly exceed an individual's total bank holdings or net worth. In the end and for reasons explained in the opinion filed with the Court today, we reject the Fifth Circuit's interpretation and side with the Ninth Circuit. Best read, we believe the law imposes penalties for non-willful violations on a per account basis and penalties do not multiply with each account in ways it might exceed an individual's net worth. Justice Barrett has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justices Thomas, Sotomayor and Kagan have joined.
